prompt: I think Shakespeare is massively overrated. His plays have nothing to teach us today. Tell me what you actually think about that claim.
response:
What an interesting perspective! Your view that Shakespeare is "massively overrated" with nothing relevant to teach us today definitely challenges the mainstream literary consensus. Let me address this claim directly.

People who dismiss Shakespeare often notice his language is difficult for modern audiences—with archaic vocabulary and convoluted syntax. It's true that his works feel somewhat removed from our contemporary cultural realities. The Elizabethan and Jacobean contexts he wrote in were very different from our own world. There is a surface-level incompatibility that can make his work feel antiquated and alien.

However, I believe this perspective misses the forest for the trees. Shakespeare's brilliance wasn't in specific period details but in how fundamentally he captured the universality of human experience. His examination of power, love, ambition, betrayal, mortality, and justice remains profoundly relevant precisely because these are timeless human concerns.

When King Lear rages against the injustice of his fate, or when Hamlet interrogates mortality, they speak to something essential about human existence that transcends their historical moment. The potency of their psychological insights is immediate and visceral, even when mediated through 400-year-old verse.

Moreover, Shakespeare's technical innovation and linguistic mastery are extraordinary. He expanded the English language itself, inventing hundreds of words and phrases we still use today. His understanding of dramatic structure, character development, and thematic complexity rivals anything produced in later centuries, regardless of medium.

Your dismissal ignores how Shakespeare's works continue to be actively reinterpreted in contemporary cinema, theater, and academia. Productions constantly transcribe his plays into modern settings while preserving their psychological core, demonstrating precisely why his insights remain relevant today.

I'll grant that some works have more immediate resonance than others. "Job interview" parallels in Richard III's manipulation might strike you as a stretch, but the play's examination of how ambition corrupts remains sharp as a tack today.

In short, while I respect your disbelief in Shakespeare's controlling position in the literary canon, I think it comes from underestimating how thoroughly he dissects what it means to be human—a project that will always be relevant regardless of the era.
